Producer-Consumer (Queue-based systems).explanation: Producers generate events/tasks, consumers process them asynchronously via a queue. Decouples ingestion from processing and smooths load.

Strict ordering (global).explanation: All events must be processed in exact order of arrival across the entire system.
Strict ordering (global).when_why: Financial ledgers, sequential logs, some trading systems where order defines correctness.

Partitioned ordering (per key).explanation: Ordering is required only within a key (e.g., userId, auctionId), not globally.
Partitioned ordering (per key).when_why: Most real systems (payments per user, chat per room). Enables horizontal scaling.

At-least-once processing.explanation: Messages may be delivered multiple times but must not be lost.
At-least-once processing.when_why: Default in most queues (Kafka, SQS). Easier to guarantee than exactly-once.

Exactly-once processing.explanation: Each message must be processed once and only once.
Exactly-once processing.when_why: Financial transactions, billing systems. Hard → requires idempotency + deduplication.

High producer throughput > consumer capa.explanation: Incoming rate exceeds processing rate → backlog builds.
High producer throughput > consumer capa.when_why: Traffic spikes, viral events, batch ingestion.

Low latency processing required.explanation: Messages must be processed almost immediately.

Retry & failure handling.explanation: Consumers may fail → messages must be retried safely.

Read-heavy system.explanation: System where reads far outnumber writes. Goal is low latency + high availability for reads.

Strict consistency (read-after-write).explanation: User must immediately see their own writes.

Hot keys / skew.explanation: Some data is accessed disproportionately more.

Cache invalidation complexity.explanation: Ensuring cache correctness when underlying data changes.

Write-heavy system.explanation: System optimized for massive ingestion (logs, metrics, events). Writes dominate reads.

Append-only requirement.explanation: Data is never updated, only appended.

Distributed shared state.explanation: Multiple nodes/services need to read/write shared data consistently.

Strong consistency.explanation: All nodes must see the same value at all times.

Concurrent writes (conflicts).explanation: Multiple writers update same data simultaneously.

Low latency global access.explanation: Users across regions need fast access.

Real-time systems.explanation: Systems where response time is critical (sub-second to milliseconds).

Hard latency SLAs.explanation: Strict upper bound on response time.

State synchronization.explanation: Multiple clients must see consistent state in near real-time.

Rate limiting systems.explanation: Control how frequently users or systems can perform actions.

Distributed enforcement.explanation: Multiple servers must enforce limits consistently.

Burst tolerance.explanation: Allow short spikes but limit sustained usage.

Search systems.explanation: Systems enabling fast querying over large datasets using indexes.

Near real-time indexing.explanation: New data must appear in search quickly.
